1: "Potiphar, an Egyptian who was one of Pharaoh's officials, the captain of the guard, bought him from the Ishmaelites who had taken him there" — the title "captain of the guard" (śar haṭṭabbāḥîm, chief of the slaughterers) designates the head of the palace guard or executioner corps. The word ṭabbāḥ (literally, slaughterer/cook) refers in the ANE context to the executioner corps who were also responsible for royal food preparation — a combination of roles that gave the position access to both the king's body and his table. Egyptian parallels include the "chief of the bodyguard" title attested in New Kingdom texts. Potiphar's name (pôṭîpar) is probably the Hebrew rendering of an Egyptian name meaning "he whom Re gave" — the theophoric element pointing to the sun deity Ra.
2: "The LORD was with Joseph so that he prospered, and he lived in the house of his Egyptian master" — the phrase "the LORD was with Joseph" (wayhî YHWH ʾet-yôsēp̄) is the theological thesis of the Joseph narrative. It appears four times in this chapter alone (Genesis 39:2, 3, 21, 23) as a refrain interpreting all of Joseph's circumstances — including the bad ones — through divine accompaniment. In ANE religious thinking, a deity's "presence" or "favor" with a person was recognized through observable outcomes: prosperity, success, and others' recognition. The Egyptian master will recognize YHWH's presence through Joseph's results (Genesis 39:3).
3: "When his master saw that the LORD was with him and that the LORD gave him success in everything he did" — Potiphar, a polytheist, recognizes Joseph's God through the evidence of Joseph's success. The narrator does not indicate how Potiphar knows which deity is responsible — whether Joseph explains it or whether Potiphar simply attributes Joseph's unusual competence to divine favor in general ANE terms. Either way, the Egyptian master's recognition of YHWH is a recurring element: a non-Israelite recognizing the God of Israel through the lives of his covenant people (cf. Rahab in Joshua 2:9-11; Ruth's declaration in Ruth 1:16).
4: "Joseph found favor in his eyes and became his attendant. Potiphar put him in charge of his household, and he entrusted to his care everything he owned" — the role of household steward or major-domo was the highest domestic position available to a slave in the ANE. Egyptian administrative records from the Middle Kingdom and Second Intermediate Period (ca. 2000-1600 BCE) document foreign slaves rising to senior household management positions; tomb paintings and administrative papyri show foreigners — Canaanites, Nubians, Syrians — in managerial roles in elite Egyptian households. The historical background is consistent with the period traditionally associated with the Joseph narrative.
5: "The LORD blessed the household of the Egyptian because of Joseph" — the blessing transferred to the surrounding household through Joseph echoes the pattern of Genesis 12:3 ("all peoples on earth will be blessed through you"). The specific beneficiary of YHWH's blessing here is a pagan Egyptian official — a preview of the broader blessing pattern that will culminate in Joseph sustaining all of Egypt and surrounding nations during the famine. The blessing "both in the house and in the field" covers the totality of Potiphar's economic interests.
6: "Joseph was well-built and handsome" — the same phrase used to describe Rachel (Genesis 29:17: "lovely in form and beautiful"). Physical beauty in the OT narratives is regularly a marker for narrative significance and often triggers narrative complications: David's beauty attracts Saul's attention and Bathsheba's situation; Absalom's beauty is noted before his political ambition. Joseph's physical appearance immediately precedes the seduction attempt, establishing the causal context. The narrator withholds the seduction setup until after establishing that Potiphar trusts Joseph completely — the simultaneous elevation of Joseph and introduction of his attractiveness sets up the crisis.
7: "After a while his master's wife took notice of Joseph and said, 'Come to bed with me!'" — the episode has a parallel in the Egyptian "Tale of Two Brothers" (Papyrus D'Orbiney, ca. 13th century BCE), in which a younger brother named Bata refuses his older brother Anubis's wife's sexual advances and is falsely accused of assault. The parallel does not prove direct literary dependence; rather, it demonstrates that the narrative type — false accusation by a woman rejected in a seduction attempt — was a known story pattern in Egyptian culture that Joseph's audience may have recognized. The woman's directness is striking: no pretense or courtship, simply a command.
8: "But he refused" — Joseph's resistance is immediate. His explanation is multi-layered: first, loyalty to Potiphar ("my master does not concern himself with anything in the house; everything he owns he has entrusted to my care") — a business/stewardship argument; then a boundary argument ("No one is greater in this house than I am; my master has withheld nothing from me except you") — a limit defined by Potiphar's marriage rights; then a covenant argument (Genesis 39:9: "How then could I do such a wicked thing and sin against God?"). The three-tier reasoning moves from social obligation to legal boundary to theological principle.
9: "How then could I do such a wicked thing and sin against God?" — Joseph's climactic argument frames adultery not primarily as an offense against Potiphar but as sin against God (ʾĕlōhîm). This is remarkable in context: Potiphar is an Egyptian, YHWH is not Potiphar's God, and no Mosaic legislation yet exists to frame adultery as a divine prohibition. Joseph's appeal to a theological rather than merely social or legal standard elevates the refusal beyond prudential calculation. David will use the same frame in Psalms 51:4: "Against you, you only, have I sinned" — though in David's case the sin was actual, not resisted.
10: "She spoke to Joseph day after day" — the repeated solicitation over time demonstrates that Joseph's resistance was not situational but principled. The phrase "he refused to go to bed with her or even to be with her" is the strongest possible avoidance: not just refusing the specific act but withdrawing proximity that could create opportunity. The daily repetition establishes that this is not a one-time crisis but a sustained pattern of temptation and resistance.
11: "One day he went into the house to attend to his duties, and none of the household servants was inside" — the empty house is the occasion that the woman exploits. Joseph's presence is professional (attending to his duties), not sought-after; the absence of witnesses removes the social deterrent. The situation is the ANE legal equivalent of a honey trap: the woman uses the physical circumstance that she has either arranged or recognized as an opportunity.
12: "She caught him by his cloak and said, 'Come to bed with me!' But he left his cloak in her hand and ran out of the house" — the cloak (begeḏ, garment) seized and retained becomes the false evidence. This is the second time a garment becomes pivotal evidence in the Joseph narrative: in Genesis 37:31-33, the brothers dip Joseph's long robe in goat's blood as proof of death to show Jacob. Here, the abandoned cloak becomes the proof of assault that Potiphar's wife will display. The garment-as-evidence motif runs through the Joseph chapters (37, 39, 44) as a recurring narrative thread.
13: "When she saw that he had left his cloak in her hand and had run out of the house" — Potiphar's wife's response is immediate and calculated: she uses the physical evidence before her story can be challenged. Her cry to the household servants (Genesis 39:14) creates a public record of her accusation before Joseph can offer any counter-narrative. The speed of her response suggests premeditation or rapid opportunism.
14: "Look, this Hebrew has been brought to us to make sport of us!" — Potiphar's wife's accusation uses the ethnic/status marker "Hebrew" (ʿiḇrî) to distance Joseph from the household. "Hebrew" in the OT tends to be used by outsiders or in situations emphasizing foreignness and lowly status (cf. Jonah 1:9; Exodus 1:15-16). Her framing — "brought to us" — emphasizes his slave status and implies the household was victimized by taking him in. "Make sport of us" (liṣḥaq bānû) — the same root used for Isaac's name (ṣāḥaq) and for what Ishmael was doing to Isaac in Genesis 21:9 — is an ironic echo.
15: "When he heard me scream for help, he left his cloak beside me and ran out of the house" — Potiphar's wife constructs a coherent false narrative: approach, assault, scream, flight, abandoned garment. The cloak is the physical evidence that makes the account credible; without it, her word against his. With it displayed beside her (Genesis 39:16), she has a material witness to the encounter that Joseph cannot explain away without access to her and the witnesses she has already briefed.
16: "She kept his cloak beside her until his master came home" — the cloak on display until Potiphar's return is evidence management: she ensures the material proof is visible and fresh. The time between the incident and Potiphar's arrival creates a period during which the servants' accounts have been given, her emotional state has been established, and Joseph has had no opportunity to present a defense.
17: "That Hebrew slave you brought us came to me to make sport of me" — the accusation is repeated, this time to Potiphar with an added implication: "that you brought us" — subtly blaming Potiphar for the situation. The accusation is socially calibrated: calling Joseph a "Hebrew slave" reasserts his status inferiority; attributing the situation to Potiphar's choice to bring him in creates a shared grievance between husband and wife against the slave.
18: "But as soon as I screamed for help, he left his cloak beside me and ran out of the house" — the identical narrative for the second time establishes the story in Potiphar's mind before he can speak with Joseph. The consistency of the account (same details to servants and husband) gives it the appearance of reliability. Whether Potiphar fully believes his wife or suspects but cannot act otherwise without losing face is not resolved by the text.
19: "When his master heard the story his wife told him, saying, 'This is how your slave treated me,' he burned with anger" — Potiphar's anger is noted but its object is ambiguous in the Hebrew: "he burned with anger" without specifying anger at whom. Some interpreters argue the anger was directed at Joseph; others suggest the anger may have been partly at the whole situation (including his wife's behavior). What is notable is that Potiphar does not execute Joseph — which he could have done and which was the expected response to an assault on the master's wife in the ANE. Instead, Joseph goes to prison.
20: "Joseph's master took him and put him in prison, the place where the king's prisoners were confined" — the royal prison (bêt has-sōhar) is distinct from ordinary imprisonment; sōhar appears only in the Joseph narrative in the OT. Egyptian "prisons" of this period were often labor compounds where offenders worked under supervision; the designation "where the king's prisoners were confined" indicates a facility for political detainees and court offenders — placing Joseph in proximity to the court officials whose dreams he will interpret in Genesis 40. The choice of prison over execution may reflect Potiphar's private doubt about his wife's account, or simply that enslaved people's lives were commodities he chose to preserve.
21: "The LORD was with him; he showed him unfailing love (ḥeseḏ) and granted him favor in the eyes of the prison warden" — the ḥeseḏ vocabulary — covenant faithfulness, steadfast love — applied to Joseph's prison situation is the same vocabulary used for YHWH's covenantal fidelity in the Psalms and the ancestral narratives. Even in prison, Joseph experiences divine ḥeseḏ enacted through practical favor. The pattern of Genesis 39 repeats at a lower level: as Joseph rose in Potiphar's house, so he rises in the prison — "the LORD was with Joseph" functions as the structural backbone of the whole chapter.
22: "The warden put Joseph in charge of all those held in the prison, and he was made responsible for all that was done there" — the structural parallel with Genesis 39:4 (Potiphar putting Joseph in charge of his household) is exact: slave → stewardship → comprehensive responsibility. The same pattern that characterized Joseph's rise in Potiphar's house repeats in the prison. The Joseph narrative uses this recursive structure to demonstrate that Joseph's competence and divine favor operate consistently regardless of the social setting: household, prison, ultimately palace.
23: "The warden paid no attention to anything under Joseph's care, because the LORD was with Joseph and gave him success in whatever he did" — the final verse of the chapter closes with the same statement it opened with (Genesis 39:2-3): the LORD's presence producing recognizable success and the superior's withdrawal of close oversight. The chapter forms a chiastic structure around the divine-presence refrain: Joseph elevated → tempted → falsely accused → imprisoned → elevated again. In each location, YHWH's presence is the constant that makes the elevation possible.
